author: @thedevilinthecircuit
text: Fascinating stuff.  This is precisely how a magnetic pickup works in an electric guitar.  One way to really boost your power generation is to place as many coils as possible under the base disk and connect them all in series, or connect pairs in series and then connect those series pairs in parallel with the other pairs, etc.  The critical consideration for multiple coils is they must be properly placed, and the spacing of the magnets must be correct so the AC pulse in each coil is as synchronized as possible.  This creates constructive phase interference in the AC phase relationships of the coils to one another.  If the spacing is off and one or more coils fires out of sync with the others, that creates destructive phase interference and robs you of power.

author: @Choober65
text:
Make the lower rotating plate wider than the blades and mount magnets top and bottom then use double the coils. Put some in series and some in parallel. Use an oscilloscope to phase the coils properly you should be able to get a couple of amps and a good voltage which can then charge a battery bank which drives an inverter.
Your magnet spacing will make a HUGE difference to the output. Having them too close causes the "incoming" field onto the coil to cancel out the "outgoing" which then causes a braking effect (Google Telma brake).

What is the orientation on the magnets on the rotor? My gut tells me they should be with the N-S axis in the direction of rotation to maximise the rate of change of flux, but it depends on the relative gaps and spacings. I suspect you've put them with the N-S axis in line with the blades. Like NW says. you should try to sketch the flux line paths at different magnet and E core orientations.

I think you've got more than a lifetime's work doing it trial and error. It used to take me days to design shielded loudspeaker magnets for use with home theatre CRT colour TVs and I had the use of 3D magnet design software! Neo magnets would be more powerful per unit volume too.

Again from loudspeaker motor design it's the mass of copper that you can squeeze into the magnetic gap which maximises the output for a given flux rate of change.

author: @trueriver1950
text:
3:04 Comments from a physics perspective. 

1. Yes, the magnets have to mover across the poles of the transformer core -- passing a leg then a gap then the centre core, then a gap then the other leg. With the lid on the transformer core, most of the changing flux cannot get into the inside of the coil and has no effect.

2. The ideal spacing of the magnets would be with their centres separated by the gap between the centres of the two slots. That is because as a North moves across the centre stub, there will be a South moving across both the outer legs. because of the shape of the transformer core, the flux from the two Souths gets turned around and all three magnets re-inforce each other. So the tip would be to measure that distance between the centres of the gaps, and then measure the perimeter around which the magnets will be placed, and divide one by the other. Then the ideal number of magnets would be the nearest even whole number to that result (has to be even as you want to alternate Souths and Norths).

3. Having the coil a bit bigger than the magnet length is actually good, as the extra width will catch some of the flux from the magnets that spills out sideways. 

4. It is a property of transformers that volts times amps is the same regardless of turns. Therefore it is exactly as the physics predicts that you got the same Wattage regardless of which coil you used,  if we ignore the resistance of the coil. It is good to see this experimentally verified by someone who didn't know what to expect and therefore couldn't fiddle the results to fit theory.  Use whichever best fits the voltage your electrical system can cope with. 

Hope that all helps your next build as much as your DIY is helping me  --  I may know the physics but the practical side, for me, is the hard bit.

author: @TheRebelmanone
text:
Depending on your need will depend on if you use heavy thicker coils with the 2v output or the thinner coils with 20v output. We realize they both produce approximately the same amount of energy, but depending on if you need to transmit the energy thru long runs of wires to get to your destination, or if your run is short to your destination, is one consideration in how to determine which coil size to use. 

 Less energy loss as heat in the transmitting of energy using higher voltage. So if you have a long run to go then it might benefit you to use the higher voltage coils, like 20 coils at 20v=400v. Then drop the voltage using transformer right next to your batteries. Or if you have a short run to the batteries, then 2v at 6 coils = 12v but you can still have around 20 coils, either use 18 or 24 coils to have either 3 sets of 12v outputs, or 4 sets of 12v output from the turbine. It is in how they wired, you can still get the voltage you want. Like if you don't use 12v batteries or if your have 12v batteries in series to make either 24v or 48v. You can still do the same to match what you will need with the coils and how they are wired. Instead of 4 sets of 12 volt outputs with 24 coils, you can wire them to be 2 sets of 24v outputs, or 1 set of 48 volt output.

author: @ZsOtherBrother
text:
Looking at the video it suddenly occurred to me why turning the iron core "sideways" worked better...
Because of the magnets' polarity arrangement: N-S-N-S... when you put a "W" shaped core sideways, you get one N or S polarity in the center and two of the opposite polarity on the sides, maximizing the magnetic flux around the coil.
Turned 90 degrees, the core's isolated sheets prevent the flux from flowing between poles (magnets), and you get only the net effect of the magnets on the part of the coil that is perpendicular to the movement, or close to it.
This also explains why removing the top of the core improved the performance: the top allowed magnetic flux to flow "over" the coil, rather than taking the long way around it, thus removing some (or most) of the flow that is perpendicular to the coil's wires, and reducing the interaction between them.

If this is true, you can probably improve it even more by spacing the magnets in a way that fits the distance between the core's "legs" (and gaps between cores), so that they don't cancel each other.

This is fun, thanks :)

author: @JehuMcSpooran
text: I've been thinking about this problem for quite a while now actually. Thinking back to my Basic Magnetics module at tafe, 3 things influence voltage output, flux density, i.e. the amount of flux lines between two magnets, the length of the conductor cutting through the field, hence why we coil it up, and the speed of the conductor through the magnetic field. Thickness of the conductor will affect the amount of current allowed to flow. Now we could vary the length of the conductor by switching on coils in series or take taps off coils but that would be more of a digital solution which could have a jarring, stepping effect on the output and may or may not be harder to control depending on the setup. We can't really change the speed or the conductor through the field as that is dependant on the angle of attack of the rotor blades and the wind speed unless we have a variable blade pitch setup. The option I have been thinking about of the past few months is to change the field strength. How do we do that? Well you were holding it in your hands. The bolt is perfect as it is a screw which can be rotated in and out of the core of the coil. This is done all the time in arc welders. this could also be done automatically by the rotor itself. How? remember those rotational governors with the two brass balls on old steam engines? They could be used in the same way here. As the rotor spins the governor, the balls are thrown outwards thus causing the arms to push a central rod that moves the coil core in or out, adjusting the core strength depending on the RPM of the rotor. Hope this helps.

author: @localbroadcast
text: make the gap between the transformer core material and the magnets smaller and you will see more output power guaranteed.  Home made generator setups like this tend to have big drops in efficiency at this gap because most people can't build machines with a high level of precision.  Also they may not realize how much induction decreases when the air gap between the magnet and the coils is increased.  On that note, if you could get transformer cores which were able to line up with the North and South magnets at the same time (North pole magnets aligned with the outer legs of the "E" and the South pole magnet aligned above the center leg of the "E" core) and you'll again see an order of magnitude increase in output or possibly even more.  If the magnets aren't correctly aligned with the legs of the core, then its like at some points in the rotor's rotation, you'll have destructive interference between some of the magnets, giving a weaker output.  You'd probably be better off to just keep the center core of the coil and remove the outer legs of the 'E' if it isn't aligning with your magnet spacing.

author: @gspin96
text:
I think i can explain why you have better results with one orientation of the coil compared to the other.

When you have magnets passing by parallel to the long side of the coil, you might have two opposing magnets over the core at the same time. 
The voltage depends on how quickly the *total  magnetic flux* through the middle of the coil changes over time. If you have two opposing magnetic fields going through at the same time, they will cancel each other out, and reduce output. 

So you'd want the magnets to be entirely covered by the coil, and only one polarity at a time, the latter of which is achieved by having the magnets moving through parallel to the short side of the coil.

author: @danielbostrom129
text: Use the core! For a given power output, you will need far less coils because as you noted the core focuses the magnetic flux. The slowing down of the turbine was just how the turbine develops the torque needed to supply the power for how much you are drawing. When there is no load, the turbine will accelerate to (nearly) the speed of the wind, but at that speed you are not extracting much energy from it. For a given wind speed the energy that can be extracted increases with a decrease in turbine velocity due to an increase in angle of attack, until the angle of attack reaches the critical angle of attack at which point the blades will stall. How much it will slow down before stall will have to be determined experimentally by adding more and more load until the power output suddenly drops and the turbine suddenly slows dramatically (this could be done by adding more coils and slowly adding more load to each in turn). I would guess that the ratio of max load rpm to no load rpm would probably be the same at different wind speeds, but again, that would have to be determined experimentally. It could also be a set rpm drop. experiment and see! Great video series!

Where B=magnetic field, A=Inside Area of coil, f=frequency of flux change and N=number of turns in coil.
To maximize V you need high flux, large area coil, spin wheel faster and maximize the turns in the coil.
If you dont have a core then the "B" value will be low so voltage (and power) will be low.

author: @hanslepoeter5167
text:
Pretty much everything you said in this video makes sense.
However :
when you have 2 coils on the core you should double the power result ( at same RPM ) and certainly not cancel out.

Having 2 coils on the core is basicly no different than having two coils on 2 cores at phase equal distance.

Same thing with the RPM. You have all the magnets on the outside. However if you have a gear doubling that you could do with half the magnets on another wheel. If you have a gear making that 10 fould you end up with only a few magnets. You see ? It's just the number of magnets passing underneath per second that matters. If you only have a few magnets, the equal amount passing per second you are very close to what a commercial generator would look like.


As you said RPM will drop at electrical load which is a good indication you are actually transfering energy from mechanical to electrical. As I told you before :if you short it mechanical load on the system is maximal and you certainly should notice that in RPM. All energy is lost in the coil internal resistance. It's analog to a battery. Short it and maximum energy i s produced by the cell but all is lost in the internal resistance. ( And it gets hot :-) )

author: @rty1955
text:
Two coils of wire are needed. The coils are wired in series. The need to be placed so that each coil is under each magnet at the same time so if your magnets are. 10mm apart, so must your coils. The coils must lay flat or in a magnetic core that becomes magnetized as the flux rises and falls with each passing of the magnet. The core can be a "u" shape but each pole of the core must align with the spacing of rhe magnets. You can mold core out if magnetic powder and a resin or make them up as laminates as your transformer is.
There are many generator designs in the web
You can also wrap wire around a bolt. The ends of the bolts need to be very close to the magnets. This is crucial. Wind all coils in same direction. You can connect every other coil in parallel for more current. The voltage will depend on flux density and amount of turns on the coil. So once every other coil is wired in parallel you will have two coils sets. Lets call the. A & B. You then wire A & B in series so you wind up with 1 wire from set A and 1 wire From set B. Those are your alternating current output. Of course wiring all the coils together will induce a load on the drum. Like I said before yoy can use an arduino to control how many coils are in circuit thus controll speed that way. In high winds all the coils are "online" and in slower winds only a couple of coils are "online" you can use one coil as a tach to determine speed.

author: @headlessxp4948
text:
I know that probably, nobody will read this comment, but I still feel urged to add my 2 cents and 3 semesters of electrical engineering knowledge to this discussion.

1. The wire diameter only decides about the maximum current the wire can carry before it melts, NOT the induced voltage. In most cases you can simplify faraday's induction law into U = turns_of_wire * change_of_magnetic_flux. 

2. I think Robert is right when he says that you have a choiche when deciding for/against an iron core. It depends on the strenght of you magnets and the amount of power you want to convert to electrical power. To achieve the same flux density through a coil without a core, you need much stronger magnets to force the magnetic field through the air. If you want to use cheap, weak magnets however, an iron core (which in most cases you get for free with the coil) can compensate for their weaker magnetic field strenght.

3. I think it would be smartest to setup the coil to be able to stop the turbine completely when shorted. If you were to make you the coil "weaker", you would effectively dictate an operating point for the turbine speed, which doesn't really make sense unless you expect to always have the EXACT SAME wind speed. By making the coil "stronger",  you can use a simple buck converter and rectifier to choose a different operating point whenever you want and however you want by just turning a potientometer. A strong coil setup would also make the use of a MPPT controller possible, which seems to be optimal for this case as the wind speed fluctuates a lot. The only disadvantage of such a "strong" coil would be some minor cogging.

author: @will_doherty
text:
Interesting video and useful experimenation. A few comments: as energy only ever changes state (so can't be made or destroyed) you can only get out of a system in total what you put into it - in this case the energy supplied by the fan is converted into mechanical (kinetic) energy by the air acting on the rotor, you are then converting the kinetic energy into electrical energy. When you apply a load to the electrical output, the energy is converted into heat, and the lower the resistance of the load, the more energy will be drawn from the rotor, so it slows. When using the coil without a core, the efficiency of the conversion of energy is much poorer, so less energy is taken from the rotor, and it slows by a lesser amount.

The reason for the core thus becomes apparent - for the same power output a much smaller coil can be used, which for the manufacturer means reduced costs, and a more compact product.

When you connected the two coils together I assume it was in series? I suspect that you had the coils mounted such that the outputs were out of phase with each other and thus acting against each other - if you tried it again whilst making sure the the windings on both coils are in the same orientation, maybe it would work as expected?

I return to a point I've previously enquired about - when you took your measurements and calculated the power output, were you measuring the voltage and current at the same time, or was the voltage open circuit, and the current short cirtuit? Thanks!

author: @geeforce3891
text: From what i remember from experimenting and studying Wind Turbines myself about 5 years ago. They talk a lot about co-efficiency and matching the right type of generator for your type of blade design. its like with your batteries,  each system has to be well matched to get the best performance out of it. Just like the electrolyte and the type of separator you use, the blade design type should work best with a particular type of generator i.e. low or high rpm etc.  I think you may need to work out the co-efficiency of your blade design at your targeted wind speed range, working out what rpm your design will give - then matching the generator type to that. There are some DIY wind turbine websites out there with calculators to help you. So it is about matching it all up to get the most efficiency. i opted not use iron core as the extra energy output didn't give me any extra efficiency with my design. it creates drag or cogging as its known, which is the slowing you've experienced.

author: @wibblywobblyidiotvision
text:
My guess WRT cores...

If you have the "top" on, the top will "short-circuit" the magnetic flux, in the same way that a turn-on-and-off-able magnetic base works, and your coils will see next to nothing (you're probably only getting some generation from the "stray" flux around the ends of the coils.  Again, if the ends of the "E" are running in line with the magnets (i.e. the three ends all under the magnet at once), you short-circuit the flux again, whereas if you have it at 90° (E-coil-E-coil-E under the magnets in sequence), you're surrounding your wires with a varying magnetic field via the flux running through the E.

There's almost certainly a variance based on the relation between the magnet size and core / coil size, and you might well have benefit in only coring the coils in the centre as you mention at the end of the video.  

Interesting though it is to experiment, this stuff is probably all explained in some ancient tomes which could save you some considerable time; it's not like you're discovering something new to humanity, but I don't have the required google-fu to extract the information from the intarwebs.  Maybe something like "generator flux density" or "generator coil size"?

author: @Cooliemasteroz
text:
I know that you are trying to show how easily and cheaply this can be done. I also know that you are not are not overly concerned about efficiency but it seems a shame not to come up with an efficient electrical side since you’ve done such a great job of the mechanical part.
Back in 1995 at an electric car convention here in Sydney I discovered that an electrical engineer invented an electric motor that he called a T - FLUX motor,  PATENT REF. - PCT / AU 94 / 0405   I don’t know if he has renewed his patent or not but it looks as though he has closed his business as he would probably have retired by now. 
This motor was very simple, the rotor consists of three arms made from flat steel 360 degrees apart and on the end of each arm was a cylindrical rare earth magnet. The windings were actually a toroid coil formed around a C shaped former and the magnets would run inside the coil like a continuous solenoid. There were three connections to the coil again 360 degrees apart and the power supply was a three phase DC square wave input. As you could imagine it was a very short motor. It was also about 90% efficient with no iron and it was a high speed motor.
I was thinking that you could use the concept as an alternator but instead of going to the trouble of winding a coil around the whole perimeter of your turbine you could wind three coils about say 15 degrees each, join the ends together, position them evenly apart and instead of having just three magnets on the rotor arms you could have 6, 9 or twelve to have the same effect of a higher speed. Then you could connect the three phases to a full wave bridge rectifier.

author: @nigelwilliams7920
text:
2. Coil vs magnet size. You will find it informative to do a doodle of the shape of the field lines of your magnet configuration for your north-south-north- arrangement.  Conceptualize both without and with the coil and its core in place. 
 Your coil extracts energy via the change in flux so you want to maximize the severity of flux change as the magnets pass the coil.  A wide coil will potentially be picking up conflicting flux inputs resulting in the windings having currents trying to flow in opposing directions at the same time. A narrower coil may be better at getting a unique current direction induced in it giving a cleaner output. 

Try positioning a Hall sensor in the gap between magnets and coil and watch the flux change as the magnets run by. More change is better.

author: @nigelwilliams7920
text:
Nice job!
Re the turbine. For a given wind velocity all turbines have a free running ( no load) speed (rpm) and a different (slower) speed at which they are extracting the maximum energy from the passing air mass.  Below that rpm they are stalled and wasting a lot of good wind. 
For a propeller blade type horizontal axis turbine at best power extraction the tip velocity is about seven times  the wind speed.  
So to get the best out of your turbine it must work around the optimum rpm/wind speed ratio.  So you need to try various power extraction regimes while measuring rpm to produce the power ( torque) vs rpm curve.  Best with a real wind, but your blower will do. 
Then you contrive a mechanical or electrical system to ensure the turbine is always operating close to optimum torque vs actual instantaneous wind speed. Moving coils closer to or away from the magnets would be one way to regulate the load. You will need a weather station wind speed meter (or a swing vane on a rheostat would do) to give exogenous wind speed info which is used to vary the load to keep rpm in the sweet-spot to maximize your power yield. 
 Looking forward to seeing the curve!

Note that it doesn’t matter what coil arrangement you have, as the whole system is hung off allowing the turbine to operate in the sweet spot. IMHO going for an efficient coil with high voltage output will be most use as it gives simpler options for conversion to DC and higher voltage AC has less transmission loss. 
As you’ve found, various coil configurations give similar outputs, so you would optimize for cheaper and simpler.

author: @mwm2929
text: The wattage would decrease when a load is placed on the coil.  Then the line loss will come into play due to the cross sectional diameter of the wire used.  The best results possible will be acheived with your generator due to the high frequency of the output.  High frequency is key to minimizing the hysteresis.  It would be ideal to cut the silicone steel plates apart.  The welds actually work against the purpose of the thing not being in one large chunk of steel.  The laminated cores of transformers are not typically welded, but lacquered to keep them electrically isolated from one another to avoid eddy currents from passing in a path other than passing through the coil.  MOTs are welded as a easy manufacturing technique.  It causes them to generate more heat because of the eddy current passing through the weld, and makes them less efficient.

author: @marklewus5468
text: Rob, the reason you get the same power out of different coils is not a result of the mass of copper. The amount of power is determined by the magnitude of magnetic flux, and how fast it is moving past the turns in the core, less eddy current losses in the core and copper (resistance x current, aka IR) losses in the windings. More or less like a transformer, except in this case the flux is applied externally rather than being generated by the primary coil. Different numbers of turns changes the voltage versus current, but power remains the same. To reduce copper (IR) loss to a minimum, the coil should make full use of whatever space there is in the core.

replies.author: @wjcferguson
replies.text:
Yep - as I remember from secondary school physics, in the ideal, number of winds determines voltage/current combo through which you'll get a fixed power.  Obviously real-world with resistance, eddy currents etc., will skew that balance, but the fundamental relationship between coils is about windings.

The transformer designers will have balanced the real world losses.  Cheapest design would be one wind on the primary, 10, or whatever the multiplier is, on the secondary.  But plainly that is inefficient so they increased the winds on each side to a point with acceptable efficiency for a given cost.

The thickness of wires is just chosen to allow them to pass the current, which is why the primary (fewer windings, lower voltage, higher current) has thick wire and the secondary has thin - (almost?) purely whatever appropriate gauge wire is needed to carry its design current.

As for whether to use a core - that just improves amount of flux it can intercept (no?) - how much kinetic energy the coil can capture and transform to electric.  The whole point of this is to extract kinetic energy and convert it.  Of course the core-less coil didn't slow the turbine as much, it was extracting 1/4 of the energy (halved both voltage and current).

If you put too much load on the turbine, it won't spin well, so a useful design needs to have limited extraction abilities one way or another.  If your scavenged parts are too powerful, perhaps it is pragmatic to reduce its efficiency, but otherwise there's not a lot better about a unit that is not able to extract as much.  Remove the load and it speeds up, right? so limit how much electricity you take from it and you can control (and therefore optimize) the turbine speed?

Finally...  connecting the two coils...  In the transformer, one is designed to take the energy input by the other - they're in opposition.  If Robert saw the output drop it probably meant he connected them in opposition to each other.  Reverse the polarity of one of the coils and they'll just give you more windings, higher voltage, lower current, same output (but probably fractionally higher from slightly lower losses?).

I'm in awe of, and salute, the practical investigations here, but getting a theoretical guy on hand (e.g. a secondary school physics teacher?) to help direct the investigations would accelerate progress dramatically.  The complement of theory nerd matched with a capable inquisitive hands-on engineer type is powerful.  They shore up each others' weaknesses and keep each other from following blind alleys.

author: @kenrowe167
text:
Hi Rob. I hope this is helpful.
The induced voltage e = -N d(phi)/dt, the number of turns times the rate of change of flux linkages.
The current i = e/Z  the complex impedance, where Z = sq.rt.(R^2+Xl^2). Xl is the inductive reactance which is proportional to L, the inductance. So lots of iron means lots of inductive reactance. What is really needed is lots of turns of thick wire (low R) and low (or no) iron (low Xl).
If you're using multiple coils then you are summing a number of waveforms which may not be in phase with each other. If you rectify each coil separately then you should be able to connect the dc outputs in series / parallel without worries.

author: @robinvince616
text: If you can get the rotation to slow significantly when shorting your coil(s) or putting a current meter or load resistor across them, that is a GOOD thing. It shows you are successfully extracting energy from the moving mass. If it doesn't slow down when working into a load, then it is not operating efficiently. In physics, you can't get something for nothing - a point the "free energy" brigade fail to grasp. And please don't get fixated on voltage or current as separate measurements or else you will be horribly wrong in your estimations of the power you are producing. You absolutely need a resistive load on which to take your measurements. By all means select the ohms value that gives you the best power reading - the optimum load will depend on many factors.

author: @hondablack1
text: Fascinating Robert. I would go as far as saying, ground breaking mate. If I might be allowed to provide some input, what about air gap ?  In the world of electrical generation, air gap, or lack of it, is quite relevant. I'm talking AC generators here, as in your average car alternator. It would seem that, in order to get the most output from such a device, then you need to construct quite a close tolerance machine with the smallest possible air gap between the rotor and the stator. This is so the magnetic circuit is as close coupled as possible. This requires accurate machining and good quality bearings to achieve a consistent and small air gap. For any given magnetic coupling there is an optimum system rpm. In vehicle applications, this equates to somewhere between 1000 and maybe 4000 crankshaft rpm. Not sure what alternator revs that  equates to but probably  four times crankshaft, so 15,000 rpm maybe. A governed wind turbine speed is going to be more useful, I feel. Not sure how this translates to your microwave transformer implementation but the rate of cutting of the lines of flux ( ie the field provided by the whirling magnets is what's important. A tuned setup is what you need, to suit your average wind speed. Then  pick your wire csa and number of turns to give required output. Hope that helps a bit. Good luck.

Now in this static position have the same size S magnate positioned the same amount of overlap into the middle part of the E and it’s other edge lined up,with the trailing edge of the E core. Does that make sense?
Now not sure what gap there should be between the N and the S magnets but I would think it’s important that both the N and S magnets side by side should not be any wider than the leading and trailing edges of the E core because if you do maybe it will lead to inefficiency? I’m just theorising here on all of this Bob but it’s based in my gut feeling from my experience in similar fields of work. 
I think that if you experiment with a fixed core size in the bench and experiment with different size magnets and spacing you should get a good result. 
Like I say, imagine the travel of the North Pole magnet followed by the South Pole magnet in such a way that a nice sinusoids magnet field is in sync with the size of the coils and their core such that the induced current produces a well shaped sinusoidal path.
If you can get hold of an oscilloscope and look at the waveform (terminate the coils with a low impedance resistance) and observe that you get a nice smooth sine wave, if the magnet sizes and position are wrong it may result in a weak amplitude or worse, misshapen sine wave. Once you have the waveform correct then try magnets with a bigger strength, neodymium for example and or the proximity. You may find that if you get your magnets too close then saturation of the core might take place resulting in back EMF fighting against the induced current ...

So a few thoughts to consider perhaps?

replies.author: @CreativeCircuits
replies.text:
Desmond Bagley I guess we are to take stock in what we are trying to achieve. More power with less wind or safer and cheaper power whatever the yields are.

There are countless ways to build wind generators and thus countless results and power yields. 

The most efficient and power dense results are to build it yourself and with neo magnets and in an open coil (no Iron) 3 phase wiring configuration. The cost increase will be determined in your ability to acquire free material or if you prefer to purchase it, it is still cheaper than buying it already built and cheaper than paying for it to be pumped into your needs. 

If you are not safe in your building these things or you do not have the sense of flowing electrons that can kill you if you touch bare conductors with bare hands then you should probably not be doing this at all. If you are not savvy enough to find material to minimize costs then you probably should never complain about costs. If it is results and performance that you want to achieve then it is most certainly conceivable to be able to do this and still do it economically because chasing a rabbit without a clear goal and without then doing the research to achieve this goal is more costly than doing it right.

You can easily achieve 1 KW generator build and still keep it in the 100 dollar range. I say generator, because if you do it right it can be used in any sort of system, wind, steam, buoyancy, water, or a bio fuel or gasifier system. It can be made to be a modular or bolt on type of generator coil set and magnet rotors to be used in any application you want it in.

The only thing you need to do is capture as much of the energy as you can from the system you are putting it in. The proper way to engineer these things is to first set out your goals or what it is you want to achieve. Then it is to make the whole of the system into compartments that address the areas of what they are to function in and make each part of the system as efficient and as energy dense as you can achieve while maintaining practicality, and then combine them.

We know that if we build a generator that produces plenty of power in low speeds then this is ideal for a wind generator and thusly for any other application that it is bolted to, as this will ultimately lower our expense in trying to capture energy that traditionally or industrially requires high speeds or high pressures. 

The lower to middle ground that can be achieved by any home builder to harness wind, steam or any other source they may have access to without the high costs of equipment that needs high power or speeds or pressures as industrial equipment does. The demands are different in the industrial environments and thus require these high cost equipment but we do not need to produce any where near the power that they do and thus the engineering changes drastically. 

We can however utilize the current technology in a much more effective way than the big industrial producers can. We can salvage much of this tech from things all around us and then design the circuits to better utilize the available energy source at a smaller scale. This is truly the real answer going forward. The answer is to have all or most of us producing our own power locally. This is much cleaner and much less expensive and much more responsible for the future and a lot less wasteful and much more reliable.

text: I think the resistance to the motion of the turbine comes from back EMF and the large amount of iron in the core interacting with the magnets. Cutting off the outer cores would reduce the magnetic brake effect while the inner iron core will improve flux flow, in particular the capture of the induced voltage as the magnetic flux collapses with the passing of each magnet. Thus the higher voltage option becomes even more attractive. That's my theory anyway. Great stuff.

replies.author: @headlessxp4948
replies.text: Sorry, but i dont think back EMF from the iron core are to blame here. Those cores are actually built to minimze back EMF. Thats the reason they are built from thin slices of isolated material, to stop any eddy currents from flowing. This means that although a voltage is induced in the core, no current can flow and no "reverse" magnetic fields can build up. Also by cutting the outer "wings" you replace a low-magnetic resistance flux path with one with huge flux resistance, which means your basically throwing away a huge amount of magnetic flux, meaning you have to buy stronger, more expensive magnets to get the same output power.

replies.author: @totherarf
replies.text: @headlessxp4948 It is a while since I did this back when I was an apprentice, but ..... The laminations are to stop eddy currents forming in the core (actual induced electrical currents that would cause heating) not to stop the magnetic flux that you want to flow (maybe flow is not the right word here) The changing magnetic flux would induce electrical flow in any metallic paths it crossed. Laminating the core limits any eddy current to flow in parallel with the magnetic flux when it wants to run at right angles to it!

replies.author: @totherarf
replies.text: I think you are right. The back EMF from a collapsing magnetic flux would induce an opposing magnetic flux which would react against the fixed magnets causing "cogging" !

author: @stuartdavies8388
text:
It is simple really.
If you want an acceptable amount of power out(100w), there will be a decent load on it that requires strong winds for it to work or it will stall. Regardless of the amount of coils or cores the load on it will be the same if its producing an acceptable amount of power(100w).
If, on the other hand you want a virtually unusable amount of power out(1w), there will be very little load, so requires light winds for it to work.
The skill is to make it efficient enough to produce a useable amount of power(100w). For that I suspect you will need much larger blades to catch the power of the wind, and a decent MPPT controller to make the best use of the power it produces.
